enrichment.short_description: Cal.com is a developer-friendly scheduling platform for booking meetings, syncing calendars, and automating team availability, used by individuals through enterprises.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Cal.com is a scheduling platform with unlimited event types, round-robin booking, and 100+ integrations. See 2026 pricing, features, and pros/cons.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Cal.com is a scheduling and booking platform founded in 2021 by Peer Richelsen and Bailey Pumfleet, initially launched as Calendso before rebranding after acquiring the cal.com domain later that year. The company is headquartered in San Francisco and employs roughly 40 people. Cal.com raised approximately $32.4 million in funding, including a $25 million Series A led by Seven Seven Six in April 2022 that valued the company at $150 million; other investors include OSS Capital. Cal.com was built on a commercial open-source (COSS) model, combining a free self-hostable codebase with paid hosted cloud and enterprise tiers, and it grew a large developer community around its AGPL-licensed repository. In April 2026, Cal.com announced it was moving its core hosted product from an open-source license to a proprietary one, citing concerns that AI-assisted code scanning made publicly visible source code easier for attackers to exploit; the company said it would keep security-critical components open under a new MIT-licensed project called Cal.diy, describing the move as a continuation rather than an abandonment of its open-source roots. The decision drew criticism from parts of the open-source community, including skepticism about whether security was the primary motivation. Functionally, Cal.com lets users and teams create unlimited event types and booking pages, supports collective and round-robin scheduling for teams, and offers workflow automation, custom notifications, and routing forms for qualifying inbound leads. It integrates with over 100 apps, including Google Calendar, Outlook, Zoom, Stripe, PayPal, Salesforce, and HubSpot, and exposes an API and embeddable widgets so developers can build scheduling directly into their own products. Native iOS and Android apps, branded 'Cal.com Companion,' let users manage bookings and receive push notifications on mobile devices; the iOS app requires iOS 15.1 or later. Pricing has four tiers: Free ($0, one user, unlimited event types and calendars, 100+ integrations, mobile app); Teams ($12/month per user billed annually, roughly 25% cheaper than monthly billing, with a 14-day free trial), which adds team scheduling, round-robin routing, managed event types, and API access; Organizations ($28/month per user billed annually, also with a 14-day trial), which adds unlimited sub-teams, SAML SSO, SCIM provisioning, custom subdomains, and SOC 2, HIPAA, and ISO 27001 compliance options; and Enterprise, with custom pricing for dedicated support, SLAs, and HRIS integrations. Cal.com competes most directly with Calendly and positions itself toward technical teams, agencies, and companies that want deeper customization, self-branding, or compliance controls than typical consumer scheduling tools provide. It is commonly used for sales demo booking, recruiting and interview scheduling, customer support scheduling, and embedding booking flows inside SaaS products via its developer platform.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Cal.com is a scheduling platform founded in 2021 by Peer Richelsen and Bailey Pumfleet, originally released as Calendso before the team acquired the Cal.com domain and rebranded. The San Francisco-based company built its reputation on a commercial open-source model, pairing a free, self-hostable core with paid cloud and enterprise plans.

In April 2026, Cal.com shifted its main hosted product to a proprietary license, citing the risk of AI tools scanning public repositories for vulnerabilities, while open-sourcing a security-focused component called Cal.diy under an MIT license. The move drew both attention and criticism from the open-source community it had built.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Cal.com supports unlimited event types and booking pages, team-based round-robin and collective scheduling, workflow automation, and routing forms for qualifying leads before a meeting is booked.

It connects to more than 100 apps, including Google Calendar, Zoom, Stripe, PayPal, Salesforce, and HubSpot, and offers a public API plus embeddable booking widgets for developers who want to build scheduling directly into their own products. Native iOS and Android apps handle bookings and notifications on the go.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Cal.com's Free plan covers one user with unlimited event types and 100+ integrations. Paid plans scale per user: Teams starts at $12/month per user (billed annually) with a 14-day trial, and Organizations starts at $28/month per user (billed annually) adding SSO, SCIM, and compliance features. Enterprise pricing is custom.

features.title: Unlimited Event Types
features.description: Create as many booking page types as needed, even on the free single-user plan.

features.title: Round-Robin & Collective Team Scheduling
features.description: Distribute bookings evenly across a sales, support, or recruiting team, or book everyone at once for panel meetings.

features.title: 100+ App Integrations
features.description: Connect calendars, video conferencing, payment processors (Stripe, PayPal), and CRMs (Salesforce, HubSpot).

features.title: Workflow Automation & Routing Forms
features.description: Trigger custom notifications and route leads to the right person based on form answers before a meeting is booked.

features.title: Developer API & Embeds
features.description: Build scheduling directly into your own product using Cal.com's API and embeddable widgets.

features.title: Enterprise Compliance
features.description: SAML SSO, SCIM provisioning, and SOC 2, HIPAA, and ISO 27001 support on the Organizations and Enterprise tiers.

features.title: Mobile Apps
features.description: Cal.com Companion apps for iOS and Android let users manage bookings and get push notifications.

pros: Generous free tier with unlimited event types for individual users
Deep customization through its API, embeds, and (formerly fully open-source) codebase
Wide integration library covering calendars, payments, and CRM tools
Enterprise-grade compliance options (SOC 2, HIPAA, ISO 27001) at the Organizations tier
cons: Moved its core hosted product from open-source (AGPL) to a proprietary license in April 2026, disappointing parts of its self-hosting community
Per-seat pricing on Teams and Organizations plans can add up for larger teams
Some advanced routing, SSO, and compliance features are gated behind higher-priced tiers

faqs.question: Is Cal.com free?
faqs.answer: Yes. The Free plan supports one user with unlimited event types, calendar connections, and access to 100+ integrations. Paid Teams and Organizations plans add team scheduling and enterprise features.

faqs.question: Is Cal.com still open source?
faqs.answer: Cal.com was originally built as an open-source (AGPL) project, but in April 2026 the company moved its main hosted product to a proprietary license, citing AI-related security concerns. It continues to maintain a smaller, MIT-licensed security component called Cal.diy.

faqs.question: What is Cal.com used for?
faqs.answer: Cal.com is used for booking one-on-one and team meetings, automating scheduling workflows, qualifying inbound leads with routing forms, and embedding scheduling into other products via its API.

faqs.question: Does Cal.com integrate with Zoom and Google Calendar?
faqs.answer: Yes, Cal.com connects with Google Calendar, Outlook, Zoom, and more than 100 other apps, including Stripe, PayPal, Salesforce, and HubSpot.

faqs.question: Is there a Cal.com mobile app?
faqs.answer: Yes. Cal.com Companion is available free for iOS and Android and lets users manage bookings and receive real-time notifications.

use_cases.title: Sales & Recruiting Scheduling
use_cases.description: Route inbound leads or candidates to the right rep or interviewer automatically using round-robin scheduling and routing forms.

use_cases.title: Embedded Product Scheduling
use_cases.description: Use Cal.com's API and embeddable widgets to add booking flows directly inside a SaaS product.

use_cases.title: Individual Meeting Booking
use_cases.description: Freelancers and professionals share a personal booking link for client calls, consultations, or interviews.

enrichment.short_description: Calagopus is a free, open-source game server management panel built in Rust for deploying, monitoring, and administering Minecraft, Rust, and other multiplayer servers.
enrichment.seo_title: Calagopus: Free Open-Source Game Server Panel
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Calagopus is a self-hosted, open-source Rust game server panel and Pterodactyl alternative. See features, licensing, and setup details.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Calagopus is a free, open-source game server management panel positioned as a modern, ground-up alternative to the widely used Pterodactyl Panel. Rather than reusing Pterodactyl's PHP-based stack, Calagopus was rewritten from scratch in Rust for the backend with a React frontend, a choice its documentation and marketing materials attribute to major gains in throughput and memory efficiency compared to PHP-based panels. Its GitHub repository (calagopus/panel) shows the project publicly active since 2025, with a steady stream of point releases (versions such as 1.0.2 through 1.0.11 as of mid-2026) maintained under the calagopus GitHub organization. The software is self-hosted: operators run the web-based panel component on Windows, Linux, or via Docker, while the server-side component (called Wings, following Pterodactyl terminology) requires a Linux host to run the actual containerized game servers. Calagopus supports ARM64 architectures, including Raspberry Pi deployments, which makes it accessible to hobbyists running small home lab setups as well as larger commercial game-hosting operators. Each managed game server runs in an isolated Docker container, which the project frames as a core part of its security model alongside general secure-web-application practices for the panel itself. Calagopus uses an 'egg' system, borrowed conceptually from Pterodactyl, to define how different games and server software are installed and configured; because Calagopus aims for compatibility with existing Pterodactyl egg presets, administrators migrating from Pterodactyl can reuse much of their existing configuration rather than starting over, and the project provides migration tooling to ease that transition. The panel is designed to be extensible, offering a plugin system built on Rust traits so developers can add custom functionality to both the frontend and backend rather than being limited to what ships in the base install. Calagopus is free for both personal and commercial use with no feature gating, no subscription tiers, and no license fees, which is consistent with its MIT-licensed core components (individual repositories in the project may carry their own specific license terms). Because it is a self-hosted, open-source project rather than a company with a commercial product, there is no publicly disclosed company size, headquarters, or funding history; support and development activity are coordinated through its GitHub organization and a Discord community server rather than a formal sales or support organization. Calagopus is aimed at game server hosting providers, Minecraft and Rust community operators, and homelab enthusiasts who want a faster, lower-resource-footprint alternative to established panels like Pterodactyl, particularly those comfortable administering their own Docker and Linux infrastructure. As a relatively young project, it has a smaller track record and community size than more established, years-old panel software, which is a consideration for operators evaluating long-term stability and support before migrating production game-hosting infrastructure onto it.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Calagopus is an open-source, self-hosted panel for deploying and managing game servers such as Minecraft and Rust. It was written from scratch in Rust and React as a modern alternative to Pterodactyl Panel, a widely used PHP-based game server management tool, with the stated goals of better performance, a refreshed interface, and a more extensible plugin architecture.
The project has been publicly developed on GitHub since 2025 under the calagopus organization, with frequent point releases. It is free for personal and commercial use with no feature gating.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Each game server runs inside an isolated Docker container, and the panel supports Pterodactyl-compatible 'egg' presets so administrators can reuse existing game installation configurations. A Rust-trait-based plugin system lets developers extend both the frontend and backend.
The panel component runs on Windows, Linux, or Docker, while the Wings server-side daemon requires Linux; the project also supports ARM64 hardware, including Raspberry Pi.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Calagopus is entirely free and open source, with no paid tiers, subscriptions, or feature gating for personal or commercial deployments. Users are responsible for their own hosting infrastructure costs.

features.title: Rust-Based Architecture
features.description: Backend built in Rust rather than PHP, aimed at higher throughput and lower memory use than older panels.

features.title: Docker-Isolated Servers
features.description: Each managed game server runs in its own Docker container for isolation and security.

features.title: Pterodactyl Egg Compatibility
features.description: Supports existing Pterodactyl egg presets and offers migration tooling for admins switching panels.

features.title: Extensible Plugin System
features.description: A Rust-trait-based plugin architecture allows custom extensions on both frontend and backend.

features.title: Cross-Platform Panel
features.description: Runs on Windows, Linux, or Docker; the Wings server daemon requires Linux.

features.title: ARM64 & Raspberry Pi Support
features.description: Runs on ARM64 hardware, making small home-lab deployments practical.

pros: Completely free for personal and commercial use with no feature gating
Rust-based backend aims for significantly lower memory and CPU overhead than PHP-based panels
Compatible with existing Pterodactyl egg presets, easing migration
Extensible plugin system for custom functionality
cons: Young project (public since 2025) with a smaller community and track record than established panels
Requires self-hosting plus working knowledge of Docker and Linux server administration
No official managed/cloud-hosted option; operators handle their own infrastructure

faqs.question: Is Calagopus free?
faqs.answer: Yes. Calagopus is free and open source for both personal and commercial use, with no feature gating or paid tiers.

faqs.question: Is Calagopus self-hosted?
faqs.answer: Yes. Calagopus must be self-hosted; the panel runs on Windows, Linux, or Docker, and the Wings server-side daemon requires a Linux host.

faqs.question: How is Calagopus different from Pterodactyl?
faqs.answer: Calagopus is a from-scratch reimplementation of a Pterodactyl-style panel written in Rust and React rather than PHP, aiming for better performance and a more extensible plugin system, while remaining compatible with existing Pterodactyl egg presets.

faqs.question: What games can Calagopus manage?
faqs.answer: Calagopus can manage Minecraft, Rust, and other Linux-containerized game servers supported through its egg system.

use_cases.title: Game Server Hosting Businesses
use_cases.description: Commercial hosting providers use Calagopus to deploy and manage many customer game servers with lower resource overhead.

use_cases.title: Homelab & Community Servers
use_cases.description: Hobbyists and small gaming communities self-host Calagopus, including on low-power ARM64 hardware like Raspberry Pi, to run their own Minecraft or Rust servers.

enrichment.short_description: Calday is a free online booking and client-management app for solo service providers and small businesses, combining scheduling, calendar sync, and automated reminders.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Calday is a free booking and scheduling app for small businesses and solo providers. See pricing, features, integrations, and who it's for.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Calday is an all-in-one service business management app that combines online booking, calendar sync, client records, payments, and automated reminders in a single tool. It is aimed at solo operators and small service businesses across categories such as one-on-one appointments, on-site visits, classes, consultations, and rentals, with common use cases spanning salons, coaching businesses, tutors, therapists, counselors, and home-service providers. Users build a booking page listing their services and prices, then share it as a link, a QR code, or an embed on their own website so clients can pick an available time and confirm without a phone call. Every appointment booked through Calday syncs automatically with Google Calendar, Outlook, and Apple Calendar, which is intended to prevent double-booking across a provider's existing calendar tools. The app logs client records that update after every visit, giving providers a running history of past appointments without manual data entry. To reduce no-shows, Calday sends automated email reminders 24 hours and again 1 hour before each scheduled appointment. Payment collection is handled through a built-in Stripe integration, allowing clients to pay when they book or after a job is completed; standard Stripe processing fees apply, and Calday does not add its own transaction fee on top. Calday is available as a web app and as native mobile apps, including a listing on the Apple App Store (Calday: Booking & Scheduling, requiring iOS 15.1 or later) and on Google Play (marketed as a 'Client Booking App'), so providers and, in some cases, their clients can manage bookings from a phone. Pricing follows a simple two-tier freemium model: a Free plan aimed at small businesses that is free forever with no credit card required and no trial timer, and a Premium plan aimed at growing businesses priced at $12 per month. Calday advertises itself as month-to-month with no long-term contracts and no cancellation fees, allowing users to downgrade or cancel at any time from their account settings. Public information about the company behind Calday — such as its founding year, headquarters, funding, or team size — is limited, and the product is primarily documented through its own marketing site, help center, and app store listings rather than press coverage or funding databases, which is typical of a smaller, bootstrapped scheduling tool rather than a venture-backed company with public disclosures. Compared to larger, more established scheduling platforms like Calendly and Cal.com, Calday positions itself specifically around the needs of individual service providers and very small teams rather than enterprise scheduling, sales pipelines, or developer-facing APIs, with its feature set concentrated on booking, calendar sync, reminders, lightweight client management, and payment collection rather than advanced routing, team round-robin scheduling, or compliance certifications.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Calday is a booking and client-management app built for solo service providers and small businesses — think consultants, tutors, therapists, coaches, salons, and home-service businesses. It bundles a shareable booking page, calendar sync, client records, automated reminders, and payment collection into one tool.
The app is available as a web app and as native iOS and Android apps, letting providers manage their schedule from a phone as well as a browser.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Providers create a booking page with their services and prices, then share it via link, QR code, or website embed. Every booking syncs automatically with Google Calendar, Outlook, and Apple Calendar, and clients receive automated reminder emails 24 hours and 1 hour before their appointment.
Calday connects to Stripe so clients can pay at booking or after the appointment, with only standard Stripe processing fees applied — Calday does not add its own cut.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Calday uses a simple two-tier model: a Free plan for small businesses with no credit card required, and a Premium plan for growing businesses at $12/month. Both are month-to-month with no long-term contract or cancellation fee.

features.title: Online Booking Pages
features.description: A shareable page listing services and pricing, distributed via link, QR code, or website embed.

features.title: Calendar Sync
features.description: Automatic two-way sync with Google Calendar, Outlook, and Apple Calendar to prevent double-booking.

features.title: Automated Reminders
features.description: Email reminders sent 24 hours and 1 hour before each appointment to reduce no-shows.

features.title: Client Records
features.description: Client history updates automatically after every visit for a running appointment log.

features.title: Stripe Payments
features.description: Clients can pay when booking or after service, with standard Stripe fees and no added Calday cut.

features.title: iOS & Android Apps
features.description: Native mobile apps let providers manage bookings on the go.

pros: Free plan with no credit card required and no trial timer
Simple setup aimed specifically at solo providers and small businesses
Native mobile apps for both iOS and Android
No added transaction fee on top of standard Stripe processing
cons: Limited public information available about the company behind the product
Feature set is narrower than larger platforms like Calendly or Cal.com (no team routing, API, or advanced compliance features)
Premium features require the $12/month plan

faqs.question: Is Calday free?
faqs.answer: Yes. Calday offers a Free plan for small businesses with no credit card required, plus a $12/month Premium plan for growing businesses.

faqs.question: Does Calday sync with Google Calendar?
faqs.answer: Yes, Calday automatically syncs bookings with Google Calendar, Outlook, and Apple Calendar.

faqs.question: Can clients pay through Calday?
faqs.answer: Yes, Calday integrates with Stripe so clients can pay when booking or after the appointment; only standard Stripe processing fees apply.

faqs.question: Is there a Calday mobile app?
faqs.answer: Yes, Calday has native apps on the Apple App Store and Google Play for managing bookings from a phone.

use_cases.title: Solo Service Providers
use_cases.description: Therapists, tutors, coaches, and consultants use Calday to let clients self-book appointments and reduce no-shows with automated reminders.

use_cases.title: Small Local Businesses
use_cases.description: Salons and home-service businesses use Calday's booking page and calendar sync to manage client scheduling without manual coordination.

enrichment.short_description: Calendly is a widely used scheduling platform that lets individuals and teams share booking links, automate meeting workflows, and sync calendars across tools.
enrichment.starting_price: Free (Standard plan from $10/month per seat, billed annually)
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Calendly is a scheduling app with group events, routing forms, and workflow automation. Compare 2026 pricing plans, features, pros, and cons.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Calendly is a scheduling and meeting-automation platform founded in 2013 in Atlanta, Georgia, by Nigerian-born entrepreneur Tope Awotona, who bootstrapped the company for several years before raising venture funding. Calendly grew into one of the best-known names in online scheduling, reaching a reported $3 billion valuation around a 2021 funding round and roughly 10 million users. The company closed its physical Atlanta headquarters in July 2021 and moved to a remote-first operating model, though it remains headquartered in the Atlanta area; employee count estimates for 2026 range from roughly 380 to 545 depending on the data source, with most estimates clustering around 500. Calendly's core product lets users create single or group event types and share a personal booking link so others can pick an open time slot without back-and-forth emails; it automatically checks the user's connected calendar for conflicts. Beyond individual booking links, Calendly supports team-based scheduling features such as round-robin distribution and collective (panel) scheduling, routing forms that qualify and direct inbound leads to the right rep, and workflow automation that can send reminder emails, follow-ups, and custom notifications tied to booking events. It integrates with a broad set of business tools, including Google Calendar, Outlook, Zoom, Salesforce, and HubSpot, and offers a browser extension and embeddable widgets for adding scheduling links to emails and websites. Calendly is available as a free mobile app for both iOS and Android, accessible to users on every plan, letting them book, reschedule, and manage availability from a phone. Pricing is structured in four tiers. The Free plan supports one event type with unlimited meetings, a single connected calendar, basic integrations, booking page customization, confirmation emails, website embedding, and access to the mobile app and browser extension. The Standard plan costs $12 per month per seat billed monthly, or $10 per month per seat billed annually, and adds features suited to individuals who need more scheduling flexibility. The Teams plan costs $20 per month per seat billed monthly (for 1–30 seats), dropping to $16 per month per seat with annual billing, and is designed for teams that need round-robin and collective scheduling plus shared workflows. The Enterprise plan is built for large organizations that require single sign-on, audit logging, dedicated implementation support, or the full Salesforce routing suite, with custom, negotiated pricing based on organizational needs. Calendly is most commonly used by sales teams booking demos and discovery calls, recruiters scheduling candidate interviews, customer success and support teams coordinating client meetings, and individual professionals such as consultants, coaches, and freelancers managing client bookings, competing most directly with tools like Cal.com in the online scheduling category.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Calendly was founded in 2013 in Atlanta, Georgia by Tope Awotona and grew into one of the most recognized names in online scheduling, reaching a reported $3 billion valuation and roughly 500 employees. The company moved to a remote-first model in 2021 after closing its Atlanta office.
Calendly lets users share a personal booking link so others can pick an open meeting time based on live calendar availability, eliminating manual back-and-forth scheduling emails.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Beyond single-user booking links, Calendly supports round-robin and collective team scheduling, routing forms to qualify and direct inbound leads, and workflow automation for reminders and follow-ups.
It integrates with Google Calendar, Outlook, Zoom, Salesforce, and HubSpot, and is available via browser extension, embeddable widgets, and free iOS/Android apps.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Calendly's Free plan supports one event type with unlimited meetings. Standard starts at $10/month per seat (annual billing), Teams starts at $16/month per seat (annual billing, 1-30 seats), and Enterprise pricing is custom for organizations needing SSO and audit logging.

features.title: Single & Group Event Types
features.description: Share booking links for one-on-one meetings or group sessions based on live calendar availability.

features.title: Round-Robin & Collective Scheduling
features.description: Distribute bookings evenly across a team or schedule panel meetings with multiple attendees.

features.title: Routing Forms
features.description: Qualify and direct inbound leads to the right team member before a meeting is booked.

features.title: Workflow Automation
features.description: Send automated reminders, confirmations, and follow-ups tied to booking events.

features.title: CRM & Calendar Integrations
features.description: Connects with Google Calendar, Outlook, Zoom, Salesforce, and HubSpot.

features.title: Enterprise Security
features.description: SSO, audit logging, and the full Salesforce routing suite on the Enterprise plan.

features.title: Mobile Apps
features.description: Free iOS and Android apps available to users on every plan.

pros: Market-leading brand recognition with a large integration ecosystem
Easy to set up for individuals with a generous free tier
Strong team scheduling features (round-robin, collective, routing forms)
Enterprise-grade security and Salesforce routing for large organizations
cons: Free plan is limited to a single event type and one connected calendar
Per-seat pricing on Teams and Enterprise can add up quickly for larger organizations
Advanced routing and compliance features are reserved for the Enterprise tier

faqs.question: Is Calendly free?
faqs.answer: Yes. Calendly's Free plan supports one event type with unlimited meetings and a single connected calendar. Paid plans start with Standard at $10-12/month per seat.

faqs.question: Who founded Calendly?
faqs.answer: Calendly was founded in 2013 by Tope Awotona in Atlanta, Georgia.

faqs.question: Does Calendly have a mobile app?
faqs.answer: Yes, Calendly offers free iOS and Android apps available to users on any plan.

faqs.question: What's the difference between Calendly Standard and Teams?
faqs.answer: Standard is built for individuals with more scheduling flexibility, while Teams adds round-robin and collective scheduling for groups and starts at $16-20/month per seat.

faqs.question: Does Calendly integrate with Salesforce?
faqs.answer: Yes, Calendly integrates with Salesforce, with the full Salesforce routing suite available on the Enterprise plan.

use_cases.title: Sales Demo Booking
use_cases.description: Sales teams use routing forms and round-robin scheduling to book demos and route leads to the right rep.

use_cases.title: Recruiting & Interview Scheduling
use_cases.description: Recruiters share booking links so candidates can self-schedule interviews around interviewer availability.

use_cases.title: Client Meeting Management
use_cases.description: Consultants, coaches, and freelancers use a personal booking page to manage client meetings without email back-and-forth.

enrichment.short_description: Calibre is a free, open-source e-book library manager that lets users organize, convert between formats, edit, and sync e-books to devices like Kindle and Kobo.
enrichment.seo_title: Calibre: Free Open-Source E-Book Manager (2026 Guide)
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Calibre is a free, open-source e-book manager for converting, editing, and organizing books across Kindle, Kobo, and other devices. See features and history.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Calibre is a free, open-source e-book management application originally created by Kovid Goyal, a graduate student at the time, who launched the project on October 31, 2006. It began as a small utility called libprs500, built to work around the fact that Sony's newly released PRS-500 e-reader had no Linux support; Goyal reverse-engineered its USB protocol with help from the mobileread.com community. The tool's first purpose was converting popular e-book formats into Sony's proprietary LRF format for that reader, which was later adopted by some publishing houses. A graphical library-management interface was added next, and by mid-2008 the project had grown into a full e-book management platform and was renamed calibre — a name chosen by Goyal's wife, Krittika, with 'libre' signaling its free and open-source nature. Goyal, based in Mumbai, India, remains the primary developer, supported by what the project describes as a small core team of around half a dozen developers plus many community testers and translators across more than 200 countries; the interface has been translated into more than a dozen languages by volunteers. Calibre is built on open-source libraries and runs natively on Windows, macOS, and Linux. Functionally, calibre works as a comprehensive e-book hub: it manages a personal library with searchable metadata, converts books between formats such as EPUB, MOBI, AZW3, and PDF, includes a built-in e-book viewer and an editor for tweaking formatting, fonts, margins, and auto-generated tables of contents, and can fetch metadata and cover art for books automatically from the internet. It can also download online news sources and periodicals and convert them into e-book format for offline reading. Calibre syncs books to a wide range of e-reader hardware, including Kindle, Kobo, and Nook devices, and offers an optional content server component that can be self-hosted to give remote, browser-based access to a user's library from other devices. A recent major release (version 9.x, referenced as of 2026) added a new 'Bookshelf' library view that displays books spine-out on virtual shelves, similar to a physical bookcase. The software's extensibility comes in part from a plugin architecture that lets developers and power users add custom functionality, such as third-party plugins for tasks like machine-translating an entire e-book. Because calibre is completely free with no paid tiers, subscriptions, or licensing fees, it has no formal pricing plans, company size, or corporate headquarters in the conventional sense — it operates as a long-running, largely volunteer open-source project rather than a commercial software company. It is widely used by personal e-book collectors managing large libraries, independent and self-published authors converting manuscripts between formats for different retailers and devices, and institutions such as libraries that need a free, cross-platform way to manage and distribute digital text collections.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Calibre is a free, open-source e-book management application created by Kovid Goyal and first released on October 31, 2006, originally to work around the lack of Linux support for Sony's PRS-500 e-reader. It grew from a simple format converter into a full e-book library manager, renamed calibre in 2008.

Goyal, based in Mumbai, India, remains the lead developer alongside a small core team and a large volunteer community that has translated the software into more than a dozen languages. Calibre runs on Windows, macOS, and Linux.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Calibre manages a searchable e-book library with automatic metadata and cover fetching, converts books between formats like EPUB, MOBI, AZW3, and PDF, and includes a built-in viewer and editor for adjusting fonts, margins, and tables of contents.
It syncs books to Kindle, Kobo, Nook, and other e-reader devices, can download news sources and convert them into e-books, and offers an optional self-hosted content server for remote library access.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Calibre is completely free and open source, with no paid tiers, subscriptions, or licensing fees for any of its features.

features.title: E-Book Library Management
features.description: Organize a personal e-book collection with searchable, editable metadata.

features.title: Format Conversion
features.description: Convert between EPUB, MOBI, AZW3, PDF, and many other e-book formats.

features.title: Built-In Viewer & Editor
features.description: Read books and edit formatting, fonts, margins, and auto-generated tables of contents.

features.title: Automatic Metadata & Covers
features.description: Fetch book metadata and cover art from the internet automatically.

features.title: Device Sync
features.description: Transfer and sync books to Kindle, Kobo, Nook, and other e-reader hardware.

features.title: News & Periodical Download
features.description: Download online news sources and convert them into e-books for offline reading.

features.title: Self-Hosted Content Server
features.description: Optional server component for remote, browser-based access to a user's library.

features.title: Plugin Architecture
features.description: Extend functionality with community-built plugins.

pros: Completely free and open source with no paid tiers
Extremely broad e-book format support and conversion quality
Actively maintained since 2006 with regular updates
Cross-platform on Windows, macOS, and Linux
cons: Interface is more utilitarian than modern consumer apps and has a learning curve for advanced features
No official first-party mobile app for iOS or Android
Primarily desktop-focused; remote access requires manually setting up the content server

faqs.question: Is Calibre free?
faqs.answer: Yes, calibre is completely free and open source with no paid tiers or subscriptions.

faqs.question: Who created Calibre?
faqs.answer: Calibre was created by Kovid Goyal, who first released it on October 31, 2006, initially to add Linux support for Sony's PRS-500 e-reader.

faqs.question: What e-book formats does Calibre support?
faqs.answer: Calibre supports converting between most major e-book formats, including EPUB, MOBI, AZW3, and PDF.

faqs.question: Does Calibre work with Kindle?
faqs.answer: Yes, calibre can convert and sync e-books to Kindle devices, as well as Kobo, Nook, and other e-readers.

faqs.question: Is Calibre available on Mac and Linux?
faqs.answer: Yes, calibre is cross-platform and runs natively on Windows, macOS, and Linux.

use_cases.title: Personal E-Book Library Management
use_cases.description: Collectors use calibre to organize, tag, and convert large personal e-book libraries across formats and devices.

use_cases.title: Self-Published Author Formatting
use_cases.description: Independent authors use calibre to convert manuscripts between formats required by different retailers and devices.

use_cases.title: Institutional Digital Collections
use_cases.description: Libraries and institutions use calibre's free, cross-platform tools and content server to manage and distribute digital text collections.

enrichment.short_description: Campaign Monitor is an email marketing platform for building, automating, and analyzing email campaigns, now operated by Marigold as part of its marketing suite.
enrichment.headquarters: Nashville, Tennessee, USA (Marigold HQ); originally founded in Sydney, Australia
enrichment.seo_title: Campaign Monitor Review 2026: Pricing, Features & Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Campaign Monitor is an email marketing platform with drag-and-drop templates and automation. See 2026 pricing tiers, features, and pros/cons.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Campaign Monitor is an email marketing platform founded in 2004 in Sydney, Australia, by Ben Richardson and Dave Greiner, who bootstrapped the company around designer-friendly email tools before it grew into one of the more established players in the email marketing category. In 2017, investment firm Insight Partners merged Campaign Monitor with fellow email platform Emma to form CM Group, which then acquired Sailthru and Liveclicker in 2019 to add real-time personalization and enterprise data capabilities, expanding beyond its original small-and-medium-business focus. Further investment from Wellspring Capital Management and TA Associates in 2021 funded additional consolidation, and in 2022 CM Group merged with Cheetah Digital; in 2023 the combined company was rebranded as Marigold, a unified relationship-marketing platform. Marigold is headquartered in Nashville, Tennessee, and in 2025 sold its larger enterprise business to Zeta Global while retaining Campaign Monitor to continue serving small and mid-market customers under the Marigold umbrella. Employee estimates for the Campaign Monitor division specifically range from roughly 100 to 240 depending on the data source. Campaign Monitor's core product centers on a drag-and-drop email builder with a library of customizable templates, list segmentation, A/B testing, and campaign analytics and reporting. It has added AI-assisted email writing tools and marketing automation journeys that trigger emails based on subscriber behavior. Pricing is tiered primarily by subscriber count and monthly send volume. A Free plan supports up to 500 contacts and 500 email sends as a 30-day trial with limited reporting and signup forms. The paid Lite plan starts at roughly $13 per month for a capped number of monthly sends and includes core features like custom templates, signup forms, an AI writer, and transactional email. The Essentials plan, marketed as the best-value tier, starts at roughly $31 per month and unlocks unlimited email sends, priority support, and unlimited access to AI-powered email features, along with optional paid add-ons such as non-human click filtering and pre-built engagement segments. The Premier plan starts at roughly $171 per month (some listings show a lower promotional starting price) and adds premier-level phone support, advanced pre-built engagement segments, send-time optimization, email section locking, and a drag-and-drop website builder. An Enterprise tier offers custom, volume-based pricing with a dedicated account manager, quarterly business reviews, multi-brand account management, and consolidated billing across brands. Campaign Monitor also offers a 15% discount for qualifying nonprofit organizations and additional savings for customers who switch from monthly to annual billing. The platform is commonly used by small and mid-sized businesses running newsletters and promotional campaigns, marketing agencies managing email for multiple clients under one account, and nonprofits running donor communications, though its per-subscriber pricing structure means costs can rise quickly as an email list grows compared to some flat-rate competitors.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Campaign Monitor was founded in 2004 in Sydney, Australia, by Ben Richardson and Dave Greiner as a bootstrapped, designer-friendly email marketing tool. After a 2017 merger to form CM Group and further acquisitions and investment, the business was rebranded as Marigold in 2023, with Campaign Monitor continuing as its email marketing product for small and mid-market customers.
Marigold is headquartered in Nashville, Tennessee, after having sold its larger enterprise business to Zeta Global in 2025 while retaining Campaign Monitor.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Campaign Monitor centers on a drag-and-drop email builder with customizable templates, audience segmentation, A/B testing, and campaign analytics, plus AI-assisted email writing and behavior-triggered automation journeys.
Higher tiers add transactional email, send-time optimization, pre-built engagement segments, and a drag-and-drop website builder.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Pricing scales with subscriber count and send volume across five tiers: a limited Free trial, Lite (from ~$13/month), Essentials (from ~$31/month, unlimited sends), Premier (from ~$171/month), and custom Enterprise pricing. A 15% discount is available for nonprofits.

features.title: Drag-and-Drop Email Builder
features.description: Build campaigns with customizable templates without writing code.

features.title: AI-Assisted Writing
features.description: AI email-writing tools to draft and refine campaign copy.

features.title: Marketing Automation Journeys
features.description: Trigger email sequences based on subscriber behavior and events.

features.title: Audience Segmentation
features.description: Target campaigns to specific subscriber segments.

features.title: A/B Testing
features.description: Test subject lines and content variations to optimize performance.

features.title: Transactional Email
features.description: Send order confirmations and other transactional messages (Lite plan and up).

features.title: Send-Time Optimization
features.description: Automatically times sends for individual subscriber engagement (Premier plan).

features.title: Campaign Analytics
features.description: Reporting on opens, clicks, and campaign performance.

pros: Established platform with a long track record dating back to 2004
Easy-to-use drag-and-drop builder suited to non-technical marketers
Unlimited sends on Essentials and higher tiers
15% discount available for nonprofit organizations
cons: Pricing scales with subscriber list size, which can get expensive for larger lists
The entry Lite plan caps monthly sends rather than offering unlimited sending
Ownership changes (CM Group to Marigold, and the 2025 sale of the enterprise business to Zeta Global) have added brand confusion
Fewer advanced e-commerce automation features than commerce-focused competitors

faqs.question: Is Campaign Monitor free?
faqs.answer: Campaign Monitor offers a 30-day free trial supporting up to 500 contacts and 500 sends with limited reporting; ongoing use requires a paid Lite, Essentials, Premier, or Enterprise plan.

faqs.question: Who owns Campaign Monitor now?
faqs.answer: Campaign Monitor is owned by Marigold, a marketing technology company formed in 2023 from the merger of CM Group and Cheetah Digital. Marigold is headquartered in Nashville, Tennessee.

faqs.question: How much does Campaign Monitor cost?
faqs.answer: Paid plans start at roughly $13/month for the Lite tier, scaling to about $31/month for Essentials and $171/month for Premier, with pricing increasing based on subscriber count. Enterprise pricing is custom.

faqs.question: Does Campaign Monitor offer a nonprofit discount?
faqs.answer: Yes, Campaign Monitor offers a 15% discount for qualifying nonprofit organizations.

use_cases.title: Small Business Newsletters
use_cases.description: Small businesses use Campaign Monitor's templates and segmentation to run regular email newsletters and promotions.

use_cases.title: Agency Client Management
use_cases.description: Marketing agencies use Campaign Monitor to manage email campaigns for multiple clients from one account.

use_cases.title: Nonprofit Donor Communications
use_cases.description: Nonprofits use Campaign Monitor's discounted plans to run donor newsletters and fundraising campaigns.

enrichment.short_description: Camtasia is TechSmith's screen recording and video editing software for creating tutorials, presentations, and training videos on Windows and Mac.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Camtasia is TechSmith's screen recording and video editing software. See 2026 subscription pricing, features, and pros and cons.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Camtasia is screen recording and video editing software made by TechSmith, a private software company founded in 1987 by Bill Hamilton and headquartered in East Lansing, Michigan, on the Michigan State University Research Park since 2021, with roughly 350 employees as of 2026. TechSmith began as a custom software development firm before pivoting toward commercial screen-capture products in the mid-1990s, first with Snagit and later with Camtasia, which was first released in 2002 for Windows before a Mac version followed. Camtasia has been continuously updated since then; early versions recorded video in an AVI container using TechSmith's own TSCC codec, version 3 introduced the proprietary CAMREC container format, and version 8.4 moved to an MPEG-4-based TREC format, reflecting the software's gradual evolution from a simple screen recorder into a full timeline-based video editor. Across its history, TechSmith states that its products, including Camtasia and Snagit, have been used to create billions of images and videos by more than 73 million people in over 190 countries. As of Fall 2024, TechSmith discontinued perpetual (one-time-purchase) licenses for Camtasia, with the last opportunity to buy a perpetual license at upgrade pricing falling on October 1, 2024; all new purchases are now subscription-based. Camtasia's current pricing spans five tiers. The Starter plan costs $39 per year and provides advanced screen capture without the full editing suite. The Essentials plan costs about $179.88 per year (roughly $15/month) and adds the full video and audio editing toolkit. The Create plan costs $249 per year and layers in AI-assisted video creation tools. The Pro plan costs $599 per year and is aimed at scaled video production for teams. Enterprise pricing is custom-quoted for larger organizations; TechSmith also offers volume discounts, with reports of $140-160 per user per year for 20-50 license purchases and $90-120 per user per year for 100+ seat enterprise deployments, plus an educational discount around $169 for teachers, students, and institutions. Functionally, Camtasia combines screen and webcam recording with a timeline-based editor that supports templates, royalty-free music and icon assets, quizzing and interactive elements for e-learning content, and direct publishing to platforms like YouTube, Vimeo, and learning management systems. The newer Create tier adds AI-powered video creation capabilities. Camtasia is desktop software for Windows and macOS with no official mobile recording app, and it is most heavily used by corporate trainers and instructional designers producing e-learning content, educators and online course creators recording lecture or tutorial videos, and IT and support teams creating software walkthroughs and how-to videos, competing in the same category as tools like Loom, ScreenFlow, and OBS Studio combined with editing software.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Camtasia is TechSmith's screen recording and video editing software, first released in 2002. TechSmith itself was founded in 1987 by Bill Hamilton and is headquartered in East Lansing, Michigan, with roughly 350 employees as of 2026.
As of Fall 2024, TechSmith discontinued perpetual licenses for Camtasia, moving fully to a subscription model across its Starter, Essentials, Create, Pro, and Enterprise tiers.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Camtasia combines screen and webcam recording with a timeline-based video editor, including templates, royalty-free music and icon assets, quizzing and interactive elements for e-learning, and direct publishing to platforms like YouTube and learning management systems.
Its Create tier adds AI-assisted video creation tools, while higher tiers support team sharing and cloud storage for scaled video production.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Camtasia is priced across five annual subscription tiers: Starter ($39/year, capture only), Essentials (~$180/year, full editing), Create ($249/year, AI tools), Pro ($599/year, team production), and custom Enterprise pricing. Educational and volume discounts are available.

features.title: Screen & Webcam Recording
features.description: Capture full-screen, application-specific, or webcam video for tutorials and presentations.

features.title: Timeline-Based Video Editor
features.description: Edit recordings with a professional, multi-track timeline interface.

features.title: AI-Powered Video Creation
features.description: AI-assisted video creation tools available on the Create tier and above.

features.title: Templates & Asset Library
features.description: Built-in templates, royalty-free music, and icons to speed up video production.

features.title: Interactive Quizzing
features.description: Add quizzes and interactive elements for e-learning content.

features.title: Direct Publishing
features.description: Export or publish directly to YouTube, Vimeo, and learning management systems.

features.title: Team Collaboration
features.description: Team sharing and cloud storage for scaled, multi-editor video production (Pro tier).

pros: All-in-one recording and editing without needing separate tools
Large tutorial, template, and course-creator community with extensive learning resources
Regular feature updates since moving to a subscription model
Education and volume discounts available
cons: Perpetual (one-time-purchase) licenses were discontinued in October 2024; all new purchases are subscription-based
Pricier over time than lightweight recorders like Loom for basic screen-capture needs
Desktop-only (Windows and Mac), with no official mobile recording app
AI video creation tools require the higher Create or Pro tiers

faqs.question: Is Camtasia free?
faqs.answer: Camtasia is not free; it is subscription-based, starting at $39/year for the Starter (capture-only) plan. TechSmith previously offered perpetual licenses but discontinued them in October 2024.

faqs.question: Can I still buy a Camtasia perpetual license?
faqs.answer: No. As of Fall 2024, TechSmith discontinued perpetual license sales; Camtasia is now available only as a subscription.

faqs.question: Is Camtasia available for Mac?
faqs.answer: Yes, Camtasia is available for both Windows and macOS.

faqs.question: What's the difference between Camtasia Starter and Essentials?
faqs.answer: Starter ($39/year) includes advanced screen capture only, while Essentials (~$180/year) adds the full video and audio editing toolkit.

faqs.question: Does Camtasia have AI features?
faqs.answer: Yes, AI-assisted video creation tools are included starting with the Create plan ($249/year).

use_cases.title: Corporate E-Learning
use_cases.description: Trainers and instructional designers use Camtasia to record and edit training and onboarding videos with quizzes and interactive elements.

use_cases.title: Online Course Creation
use_cases.description: Educators and course creators use Camtasia to record lectures and tutorials and publish them to learning platforms.

use_cases.title: Software Tutorials & IT Documentation
use_cases.description: IT and support teams record software walkthroughs and how-to videos for customers and internal teams.

features: Custom-quoted for teams and large organizations

enrichment.short_description: Cannery is a free, open-source, self-hosted app for tracking firearm ammunition inventory, storage containers, and range-day usage.
enrichment.seo_title: Cannery: Free Self-Hosted Ammo Inventory Tracker
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Cannery is an open-source, self-hosted app for tracking ammunition inventory and range-day usage. See features, license, and setup details.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Cannery is a free, open-source, self-hosted web application built specifically for tracking firearm ammunition inventory. It lets users create storage containers, catalog different types and calibers of ammunition, organize inventory into ammo packs, stage rounds ahead of a range day, and record how much ammunition was used during shooting sessions, giving owners an ongoing view of their ammo levels before and after they go to the range. The project is maintained by a developer known as shibao (also referenced as shibaobun), with its primary source repository hosted on Codeberg (shibao/cannery) and mirrored on other Git hosting platforms, including a Gitea instance. Cannery is built with the Elixir programming language on the Phoenix web framework, backed by a PostgreSQL database, and is released under the GNU Affero General Public License version 3 or later (AGPLv3+), a copyleft license that guarantees the source code and any modifications remain open and available to users, including when the software is run as a network service. Because Cannery is self-hosted, there is no managed cloud version operated by the maintainer; instead, users deploy their own instance, most commonly using Docker Compose or Docker Desktop. Setup involves copying an example docker-compose.yml file, configuring environment variables such as HOST (the external URL used to generate links), DATABASE_URL (defaulting to a bundled PostgreSQL container), and a generated SECRET_KEY_BASE used to sign cookies, then starting the stack with a docker-compose command; operators typically add a reverse proxy, such as Nginx Proxy Manager, in front of the application to handle HTTPS and routing. The first user created on a new Cannery instance automatically becomes an administrator, and instance owners can choose whether to leave registration open to the public or restrict new accounts to invite tokens. Additional organizational features include custom tagging for containers and ammunition to help users build their own categorization scheme rather than being locked into a fixed structure. Because Cannery is a small, community-maintained open-source project rather than a commercial company, there is no publicly disclosed company size, headquarters, funding history, or formal pricing plan — it is free to use and free to modify under its AGPL license, with the only real cost being the server or hosting infrastructure an operator chooses to run it on. Cannery is aimed at a niche but specific audience: individual firearm owners and hobbyist or competitive shooters who want to track their ammunition inventory and usage over time without handing that data to a third-party cloud service, as well as small gun clubs or shooting groups that want a shared, self-hosted inventory system. Its self-hosted, privacy-oriented design is the core differentiator versus any consumer inventory or spreadsheet-based alternative, at the cost of requiring users to have basic comfort with Docker and self-hosting workflows to get it running.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Cannery is a free, open-source, self-hosted application for tracking firearm ammunition inventory. Users organize ammo into storage containers, log range-day usage, and keep an ongoing view of their stock without relying on a third-party cloud service.
The project is maintained by a developer known as shibao, is built with Elixir, Phoenix, and PostgreSQL, and is released under the AGPLv3-or-later open-source license.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Cannery supports custom tagging of containers and ammunition, staging rounds for a range day, and recording usage after shooting sessions. Multi-user accounts can be enabled with either invite tokens or open public registration.
The application is deployed via Docker Compose, with configuration handled through environment variables and an optional reverse proxy for HTTPS.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Cannery is free and open source under the AGPLv3-or-later license, with no paid tiers. Users only bear the cost of the server infrastructure they choose to self-host it on.

features.title: Ammunition Inventory Tracking
features.description: Catalog ammunition types and quantities across storage containers.

features.title: Storage Container Organization
features.description: Group and label ammunition by container with custom tags.

features.title: Range Day Usage Logging
features.description: Stage rounds before a range day and record what was used afterward.

features.title: Multi-User Accounts
features.description: Support for multiple users via invite tokens or open registration, with the first user set as admin.

features.title: Self-Hosted Docker Deployment
features.description: Deployed via Docker Compose with configurable environment variables.

features.title: AGPL Open-Source License
features.description: Fully open, auditable source code released under AGPLv3-or-later.

pros: Free and open source under the AGPLv3-or-later license
Self-hosted design keeps sensitive firearm and ammunition data under the user's own control
Straightforward Docker Compose deployment
Flexible tagging system for organizing inventory
cons: Requires self-hosting and basic Docker/server administration knowledge; no managed cloud version
Small, community-maintained project with limited formal support or onboarding
Narrow, single-purpose feature set focused only on ammunition tracking

faqs.question: Is Cannery free?
faqs.answer: Yes. Cannery is free and open source under the AGPLv3-or-later license, with no paid tiers.

faqs.question: Do I need to self-host Cannery?
faqs.answer: Yes. Cannery has no managed cloud version; it must be self-hosted, typically via Docker Compose, with users providing their own server infrastructure.

faqs.question: What does Cannery track?
faqs.answer: Cannery tracks ammunition inventory across storage containers, including staging rounds for range days and logging usage after shooting sessions.

faqs.question: Who maintains Cannery?
faqs.answer: Cannery is maintained by a developer known as shibao, with its source code hosted on Codeberg and mirrored on other Git platforms.

use_cases.title: Personal Ammo Inventory Tracking
use_cases.description: Individual firearm owners and hobbyist shooters self-host Cannery to track ammo levels before and after range days.
use_cases.audience: Individual firearm owners and hobbyist shooters

use_cases.title: Shared Club Inventory
use_cases.description: Small gun clubs or shooting groups run a shared Cannery instance to manage group-owned ammunition inventory.
